title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Fiji — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Instant coffee is standard
items.text: Most local cafés serve instant Nescafé rather than freshly brewed coffee. If you want proper espresso or filter coffee, stick to upmarket resorts, hotels, or dedicated specialty cafés in Suva and Nadi.

items.label: Uprising Coffee (Suva)
items.text: A rare gem serving quality single-origin coffee and local pastries. It's become a hub for expats and locals seeking authentic café culture, though it's pricier than typical Fijian establishments.

items.label: Ask for 'Fiji coffee' with caution
items.text: While Fiji grows some coffee, most served locally is imported. Request it explicitly if you want local beans, and verify freshness—many bags sit on shelves for months.

items.label: Breakfast spots open early, close by 2pm
items.text: Local bakeries and café-style shops open around 6–7am but shut down by early afternoon. Plan your morning coffee accordingly, especially outside main tourist areas.

items.label: Kokoda (or Oka)
items.text: Fresh raw fish marinated in coconut cream and lime juice, often with tomato and cucumber. It's Fiji's national dish and a staple at family gatherings and beachside restaurants—order it fresh and avoid roadside stalls.

items.label: Lovo
items.text: A traditional underground oven-cooked feast with meat, fish, and root vegetables wrapped in banana or taro leaves. Tourist resorts often stage lovo demonstrations; for authentic village versions, ask locals or join a community event.

items.label: Cassava Cake & Sweet Potato Dishes
items.text: These starchy root vegetables are Fijian staples, often prepared as boiled sides or baked into desserts. Try cassava cake as a sweet snack from local bakeries.

items.label: Palusami
items.text: Taro leaves cooked in coconut cream and often wrapped in banana leaves, sometimes with corned beef or salmon. It's rich, comforting, and widely available at local restaurants and markets.

items.label: Roti & Curry
items.text: Indian influence runs deep in Fiji; roti bread served with chicken, potato, or dhal curry is cheap, filling street food. Quality varies widely—look for busy local shops with fast turnover.

items.label: Oysters & Mud Crabs
items.text: Fiji's seafood is exceptional when fresh. Mud crabs and oysters are seasonal delicacies found at coastal restaurants and fish markets; buy them live if possible and eat same-day.

items.label: Watch out for 'island time' kitchen speeds
items.text: Menus may list dishes, but ingredients can run out or be substituted, especially outside main towns. Ask what's actually available before ordering.

items.label: 'Fresh fish' doesn't always mean caught today
items.text: Ask specifically when fish was caught—on islands, 'fresh' can mean 2–3 days old. For true fresh catch, visit fish markets early morning or eat at beachfront restaurants in fishing villages.

items.label: Portions are generous
items.text: Mains come with heaps of rice, cassava, or taro. Order fewer dishes than you'd expect, or share; you'll likely leave food on your plate at tourist-oriented restaurants.

items.label: Spice levels vary dramatically
items.text: Fijian food isn't inherently spicy, but curries and some seafood dishes are. Ask 'is it spicy?' before ordering, and don't assume chilli is included unless you see it listed.

items.label: Local Restaurants (Kana Vinaka)
items.text: Called 'kai bars' or 'local restaurants,' these serve cheap, authentic curries, roti, and grilled fish with minimal fanfare. Eat where locals eat—quality and safety correlate with busy lunch crowds.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap beachside shacks
items.text: Isolated beach bars selling overpriced fish and chips to tourists often have questionable hygiene and sourcing. Stick to established restaurants in town or beachfront resorts.

items.label: Fish Markets (Suva, Nadi)
items.text: Central markets open early morning and sell fresh whole fish, crabs, and prawns at fair prices. Negotiate prices, inspect for freshness, and ask vendors to gut/clean fish for you.

items.label: Resort Restaurants
items.text: Tourist resorts serve international fusion food and buffet spreads. Prices are 2–3x higher than local spots, but reliability, hygiene, and ambiance are guaranteed if you're risk-averse.

items.label: Street food is safe if busy
items.text: Roti carts, curry stands, and grilled corn from street vendors are fine if there's a steady queue and high turnover. Avoid anything that's been sitting under the sun for hours.

items.label: Suva, Nadi, and Labasa for variety
items.text: Larger towns have Chinese, Indian, and modern Fijian-fusion restaurants. Remote islands and villages are limited to basic local cuisine or resort food.

items.label: Kava (Yaqona)
items.text: A traditional root drink served in communal bowls, with earthy flavor and mild numbing effect on the mouth. It's culturally significant and offered at kava bars and village ceremonies; accept politely and follow local etiquette when drinking.

items.label: Fiji Bitter & Fiji Gold Beer
items.text: Locally brewed lagers that are slightly sweeter and less hoppy than Western beers. They're cheap, cold, and ubiquitous—Fiji Bitter is the stronger, darker option.

items.label: Tap water is unsafe outside main resorts
items.text: Drink bottled water everywhere except upscale hotels and resorts with good filtration. Fiji has had periodic water quality issues; stick with sealed bottles.

items.label: Fresh Coconut Water & Tropical Juices
items.text: Vendors sell fresh-cracked coconuts on beaches and in markets—one of the safest, most refreshing drinks. Passion fruit, papaya, and mango juices are common but check cleanliness of blenders.

items.label: Grog (Homebrew)
items.text: Cheap, potent locally-made spirits sold in villages and remote areas. Avoid it unless you trust the source—quality control is nonexistent and it can be dangerously strong or contaminated.

items.label: Alcohol is expensive at resorts
items.text: Imported spirits and wine cost 2–4x more at resorts than bottle shops. Buy from supermarkets or bottle-os (liquor stores) in towns if you're self-catering.

items.label: Accept food and drink graciously
items.text: If invited to a Fijian home or village, always accept hospitality even if you're not hungry—refusing is considered rude. Eat at least some of what's offered to show respect.

items.label: Remove shoes before entering homes and some sacred spaces
items.text: This applies to private residences and certain community buildings. Resorts and restaurants don't require it, but showing respect in local settings matters.

items.label: Dress conservately in villages
items.text: Avoid swimwear, short skirts, or tank tops in rural and traditional areas. Modest clothing shows respect and avoids offense; resorts and beaches are more relaxed.

items.label: Tipping is not mandatory but appreciated
items.text: Service charges are sometimes added to restaurant bills (check before paying). Small cash tips (5–10% for good service) are appreciated but not expected like in Western countries.

items.label: Kava ceremony protocol
items.text: Clap once when handed a bowl, drink it in one or two gulps, clap again, and say 'Bula!' before returning the bowl. Never refuse kava if offered at a village gathering—it's part of the welcome ritual.

items.label: Mealtimes are flexible but lunch is the main meal
items.text: Fijians typically eat a light breakfast, substantial lunch around noon, and lighter dinner. Restaurants may be quiet at dinner and busier at lunch—plan accordingly.

items.label: Avoid raw vegetables in remote areas
items.text: Stick to cooked vegetables and peeled fruit outside resorts and main towns. Washing water in remote villages may not be safe, and raw produce carries higher contamination risk.

items.label: Ciguatera fish poisoning is real
items.text: Large reef fish (barracuda, grouper, snapper) can carry ciguatera toxins from reef algae. Ask locals what's safe to eat; smaller, pelagic fish are generally safer than large reef dwellers.

items.label: Markets are open early, close by noon
items.text: Visit fruit and vegetable markets between 6–10am for the best selection and freshness. Afternoon selections are picked over and prices don't drop much.

items.label: Check expiration dates on packaged goods
items.text: Imported items sometimes sit in warehouses; even canned goods can expire. Always verify dates, especially on dairy, meat products, and medications.
